meta.title: Unit-X Institution Transformation Simulator
meta.description: This JSON file preserves the shell-compatible schema while reinterpreting the simulator around the staged Goguen-style institution for Unit-X at Wetchester. The controls represent signatures and adjustable institutional parameters; the stages represent distinct institutional forms; the Monte Carlo model estimates how sentences, models and satisfaction relations shift as demand visibility, overlap, politics, speculative strategy and redesign vary.

controls.label: Visibility of genuine value demand
controls.help: Higher values mean Unit-X distinguishes navigational, brokerage, delivery, strategic, symbolic and failure demand from the demander’s point of view.

controls.label: Speculative strategic pressure (Atom Valley logic)
controls.help: High values mean council- and leadership-led strategic initiatives dominate despite weak specification of immediate user demand.

controls.label: Platform solutionism
controls.help: High values mean the digital platform is treated as the answer to relational and institutional problems that have not yet been studied properly.

controls.label: External partnership pressure and market pull
controls.help: High values mean employers, SMEs and civic actors are actively seeking fast routes into university expertise and visible partnership capacity.

controls.label: Clarity of partner-side problem definition
controls.help: Higher values mean external partners can articulate real problems and can work productively with the university instead of expressing only symbolic or vague demand.

controls.label: Overlap with existing university innovation activity
controls.help: High values mean faculties, enterprise teams and Unit-X touch the same relationships and claims, creating duplication and ambiguity.

controls.label: Boundary-spanning brokerage capability
controls.help: Higher values mean Unit-X can interpret external demand, assemble the right people and move work across university boundaries without unnecessary handoffs.

controls.label: Delivery and support capability
controls.help: Higher values mean the university can convert brokerage into reliable project delivery, contract execution and support without heroic improvisation.

controls.label: Faculty discretion and embedded capability
controls.help: Higher values mean faculties retain recognised expertise, relationship ownership and room to respond to demand rather than being subordinated to a central unit.

controls.label: Approval layering and checkpoint bureaucracy
controls.help: High values mean innovators must pass through Unit-X or adjacent processes for permission, visibility or compliance before acting.

controls.label: Dependence on heroic individuals
controls.help: High values mean success depends on exceptional brokers or rainmakers who circumvent the formal design rather than on a reproducible system.

controls.label: Depth of consultation and co-design
controls.help: Higher values mean faculties, professional services, students, employers and civic partners help define the problem and shape redesign.

controls.label: Leadership pressure for visible innovation growth
controls.help: High values mean senior leaders prioritise symbolic visibility, big announcements and portfolio capture over careful study of demand and capability.

controls.label: Political resistance and territorial kick-back
controls.help: High values mean faculties, existing units, governance bodies or partners are resisting the unit’s remit, methods or claims.

controls.label: Governance and reputational anxiety
controls.help: High values mean compliance, reputational risk and accountability concerns are interpreted defensively, often adding controls instead of clarifying design.

controls.label: Strength of political mitigation and trust repair
controls.help: Higher values mean joint review, negative charters, transparent evidence, bounded pilots and co-governance reduce conflict and mission creep.

controls.label: Evidence-led redesign effort
controls.help: Higher values mean the institution is redesigning remit, routing, service boundaries, platform use and governance based on demand study rather than defending the launch story.

stages.name: Fragmented prehistory
stages.description: Innovation exists across faculties and embedded relationships, but it is unevenly visible and hard to navigate from outside.

stages.name: Founding catalyst narrative
stages.description: Unit-X is launched as an agile catalyst with a broad mandate, commercial autonomy claims and a fast-moving outward-facing story.

stages.name: Ambiguous parallel structure
stages.description: The unit sits beside existing services with unclear boundaries, overlapping claims and expanding interfaces.

stages.name: Politicised duplication
stages.description: Some wins occur, but they coexist with resentment, narrative inflation, failure demand and pressure to capture or approve innovation work.

stages.name: Demand-diagnostic interruption
stages.description: The institution studies demand at source, distinguishes value demand from failure demand and becomes open to redesign or even disbanding.

stages.name: Selective decomposition
stages.description: The portfolio is partitioned into brokerage, delivery, civic strategy, experimental work, handoff work and exit work.

stages.name: Reconstituted boundary-spanning service
stages.description: Unit-X becomes a narrow brokerage and delivery capability for boundary-complex demand, without universal gatekeeping.

stages.name: Adaptive civic-innovation capability
stages.description: The unit is useful, bounded and adaptive: it handles genuine boundary demand, reduces failure demand and remains legitimate because it is non-intrusive.

riskRules.title: Innovation approval trap
riskRules.body: Unit-X is likely to become another checkpoint if overlap is high and approval layering rises, increasing failure demand for people who are already innovating.

riskRules.title: Strategy-without-demand trap
riskRules.body: Speculative place-based initiatives are likely to dominate the story without a corresponding map of concrete partner demand, making Atom Valley-style work symbolic rather than service-led.

riskRules.title: Heroic dependence masquerading as design
riskRules.body: Visible wins are likely to depend on a few entrepreneurial individuals rather than on a reproducible system, which distorts learning and hides structural weakness.

riskRules.title: Territorial backlash and recentralisation
riskRules.body: Faculty resentment and governance anxiety are likely to provoke defensive control, remit grabbing or rollback unless boundaries are clarified and mitigation remains visible.

riskRules.title: Platformism is distorting redesign
riskRules.body: The digital platform is likely to be treated as a solution before the relational and organisational flow of demand has been studied properly.

riskRules.title: Surface consultation without remit change
riskRules.body: Review activity may collect views without changing the service boundary if consultation is moderate but redesign effort remains weak.

riskRules.title: Kick-back is more containable
riskRules.body: Conflict remains, but the combination of evidence-led redesign, consultation and boundary clarification makes rollback less dominant than in the parallel-unit stage.

visibility.title: Study demand at source
visibility.body: Map incoming requests, bypasses, complaints and successful workarounds. Separate genuine value demand from failure demand and symbolic or speculative demand before redesigning the unit.

redesign.title: Partition the portfolio and narrow the remit
redesign.body: Separate brokerage, delivery, strategic civic work, experimental work, handoff work and exit work. Remove functions that duplicate strong faculty capability or add needless checkpoints.

politics.title: Repair trust through explicit boundary-setting
politics.body: Use a negative charter, joint review, clear handoff rules and co-governance to reduce territorial threat and show that Unit-X is not an innovation approval authority.

clientPath.title: Strengthen partner-side problem definition and routing
clientPath.body: Help employers, SMEs and civic actors articulate real problems, then route them quickly to the right university capability. Do not let the platform substitute for relational brokerage.

S2.title: Stop conflating opportunity with demand
S2.body: At this stage the unit is too likely to treat every contact, narrative and strategic ambition as proof of need. Reclassify requests and identify what should never have entered the portfolio.

S3.title: Treat heroic wins as diagnostic signals
S3.body: The successful contracts matter, but read them as evidence of where demand is real and where the formal system is being bypassed, not as proof that the existing design works.

S4.title: Keep disbanding on the table
S4.body: A serious demand study should allow the possibility that some or all of Unit-X’s work belongs elsewhere. That possibility strengthens the discipline of the redesign.

S5.title: Protect subtraction as well as addition
S5.body: This is the stage where work must be transferred, stopped or separated. Resist the temptation to widen the unit again simply to preserve symbolic centrality.

S6.title: Preserve pull, not push
S6.body: The redesigned unit should be used where boundary complexity genuinely exists. Avoid defaulting back into portfolio capture, monitoring and mandatory routing.

S7.title: Treat adaptive legitimacy as temporary
S7.body: Continue reviewing failure demand, mission creep and subtle return of approval behaviour. The mature stage is metastable and must be reproduced through learning.

mechanismTexts.internal: function(v, summary){ return `Overlap (${v.overlapIntensity}), approval layering (${v.approvalLayering}) and platformism (${v.platformism}) are ${summary.failure.mean > 55 ? 'driving a large volume of internal failure demand' : 'less dominant than in the politicised-duplication stage'}. Raising demand visibility (${v.demandVisibility}), support capability (${v.supportCapability}) and redesign effort (${v.redesignEffort}) weakens this loop.`; }
mechanismTexts.client: function(v, summary){ return `External pressure (${v.externalPressure}) only becomes productive when partner problems are clear (${v.clientCapability}) and Unit-X can broker effectively (${v.boundaryCapability}). Where partner-side clarity is weak, speculative pressure and platformism tend to replace real demand with symbolic activity.`; }
mechanismTexts.politics: function(v, summary){ return `Political resistance (${v.politicalResistance}), governance anxiety (${v.governanceAnxiety}) and leadership visibility pressure (${v.leadershipVisibilityPressure}) ${summary.kickback.mean > 55 ? 'make remit inflation, defensive control and rollback likely unless mitigation is strengthened.' : 'remain present but are currently more containable.'} Mitigation power (${v.mitigationPower}) and consultation depth (${v.consultationDepth}) are the main stabilisers.`; }
